topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my phone, a small, sharp intrusion into the quiet hum of my evening. It was a post from Mr. Harrison, a photo of a book he’d been recommending, captioned with a thoughtful quote. My breath hitched. I hadn't seen him in person for weeks, the usual classroom interactions reduced to hurried nods and terse assignments. My fingers trembled as I tapped the screen, the image of his smiling face seeming impossibly distant. A heavy, cold weight settled in my chest, pressing down until it was hard to draw a full breath. I stared at the screen, the vibrant colors of his life juxtaposed against the dull ache blooming in my core. It felt like a betrayal, a sudden, brutal realization that the carefully constructed distance between us had dissolved, leaving behind only this unsettling, public connection. I slowly slid the phone face down, the silence of my room suddenly amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 2]

I found myself tracing the outline of his profile picture on my screen, the familiar curve of his jaw mocking my stillness. Every pixel seemed to hold a memory—the way he’d corrected my essays, the quiet intensity in his eyes during lectures. Now, it was all curated, polished, a performance I was entirely excluded from. A tightness gripped my throat, making swallowing a clumsy, painful effort. I reread the caption, searching for some hidden meaning, some justification for this intrusion. Nothing. Just a shared digital space where I was an invisible spectator to his life. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck, chilling me despite the warmth spreading through my limbs. I pushed the phone away, the plastic feeling alien in my hand. The world outside my window seemed muted, the colors leaching out of everything except the stark, hollow echo inside me.

[story 3]

The screen lit up, and there he was, smiling in a setting I couldn't quite place—a café, perhaps. It was a mundane picture, yet it landed like a physical blow. My hands started shaking uncontrollably, the slight tremor making it hard to steady my grip on the desk. I didn't move, just watched the image, my eyes blurring slightly. A profound sense of dislocation washed over me, as if the ground beneath my feet had suddenly shifted. The air in the room felt thin, insufficient. I wanted to scream, to demand an explanation, but the sound caught somewhere in my chest, trapped behind a wall of sudden, overwhelming quietude. It was a deep, internal fracturing, a feeling of something essential being ripped away, leaving behind only a raw, exposed vulnerability. I finally closed the application, the resulting emptiness echoing the cavern in my stomach.
